name: CIA Triad and Security Concepts
description: Confidentiality, integrity, and availability principles, and foundational security terminology
key_facts: Confidentiality ensures data is accessible only to authorized users (encryption, access controls)
Integrity ensures data has not been tampered with (hashing, digital signatures)
Availability ensures systems and data are accessible when needed (redundancy, backups)
Non-repudiation proves that an action occurred and cannot be denied (digital signatures, audit logs)
AAA framework: Authentication (identity), Authorization (permissions), Accounting (logging)
common_misconceptions: Encryption provides integrity (encryption provides confidentiality; hashing provides integrity)
Authentication and authorization are the same (authentication verifies identity; authorization grants permissions)

name: Zero Trust Architecture
description: Zero trust security model principles and implementation strategies
key_facts: Zero trust assumes no implicit trust; every access request must be verified regardless of location
Core principles: verify explicitly, use least privilege access, assume breach
Microsegmentation divides the network into small zones requiring separate authorization
Continuous authentication and authorization replaces one-time login trust
Software-defined perimeter (SDP) hides infrastructure from unauthorized users
common_misconceptions: Zero trust means no one is trusted at all (users are trusted after continuous verification)
Zero trust replaces firewalls entirely (firewalls remain part of a layered defense)

name: Threat Actors and Attack Vectors
description: Types of threat actors, their motivations, and common attack vectors
key_facts: Threat actor types: nation-state (APT), organized crime, hacktivists, insider threats, script kiddies
Nation-state actors are the most sophisticated with significant resources and persistence
Attack vectors include phishing, supply chain compromise, removable media, and watering hole attacks
Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities
Insider threats can be malicious (intentional) or unintentional (negligence)
common_misconceptions: All hackers are external (insider threats are among the most damaging)
Script kiddies are not a real threat (they use readily available tools that can still cause significant damage)

name: Common Attack Types
description: Technical attacks including malware, network attacks, application attacks, and cryptographic attacks
key_facts: Phishing variants: spear phishing (targeted), whaling (executives), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS)
SQL injection inserts malicious SQL code through input fields to manipulate databases
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) overwhelms targets with traffic from multiple sources
On-path attack (formerly man-in-the-middle) intercepts communication between two parties
common_misconceptions: Firewalls prevent all network attacks (application-layer attacks like SQL injection pass through network firewalls)
HTTPS prevents all web attacks (HTTPS encrypts transport but does not prevent XSS or SQL injection)

name: Cryptography and PKI
description: Symmetric and asymmetric encryption, hashing algorithms, digital certificates, and PKI
key_facts: Symmetric encryption uses one shared key (AES-256 is the standard); fast but key distribution is challenging
Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair (public/private); RSA and ECC are common algorithms
Hashing produces a fixed-length digest: SHA-256 is widely used; MD5 and SHA-1 are deprecated
Digital certificates bind a public key to an identity and are issued by a Certificate Authority (CA)
TLS uses asymmetric encryption for key exchange, then symmetric encryption for data transfer
common_misconceptions: Hashing is encryption (hashing is one-way and cannot be reversed; encryption is two-way)
Longer keys are always better (algorithm strength, implementation, and key management all matter)

name: Network Security Architecture
description: Secure network design including segmentation, firewalls, proxies, and secure protocols
key_facts: Network segmentation limits lateral movement by dividing the network into isolated zones
Next-generation firewalls (NGFW) inspect traffic at the application layer with deep packet inspection
NAC (Network Access Control) enforces security policies before allowing devices on the network
Secure protocols: SSH (22) replaces Telnet, SFTP/SCP replaces FTP, HTTPS replaces HTTP
Jump server (bastion host) is a hardened system used to access devices in a secure zone
common_misconceptions: A DMZ is completely safe (DMZ servers are still targets and must be hardened and monitored)
VPN guarantees security (VPN encrypts the tunnel but does not protect against compromised endpoints)

name: Identity and Access Management
description: Authentication methods, access control models, and identity federation
key_facts: MFA factors: something you know (password), something you have (token), something you are (biometric)
SSO (Single Sign-On) allows one set of credentials to access multiple applications
SAML, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect are federation and authorization protocols
PAM (Privileged Access Management) secures and monitors accounts with elevated permissions
Access control models: RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute-based), MAC (mandatory), DAC (discretionary)
common_misconceptions: OAuth is an authentication protocol (OAuth is an authorization framework; OpenID Connect adds authentication)
Biometrics are infallible (biometrics can be spoofed and have false acceptance/rejection rates)

name: Incident Response Process
description: Incident response phases, digital forensics basics, and evidence handling
key_facts: IR phases: Preparation, Detection/Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned
Chain of custody documents who handled evidence, when, and what was done
Order of volatility: CPU registers/cache, RAM, swap/pagefile, disk, remote logs, physical media
Forensic imaging creates a bit-for-bit copy of a drive while preserving the original
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) include unusual network traffic, unauthorized access, and file changes
common_misconceptions: Incident response starts when an attack is detected (preparation is the first and most critical phase)
Deleting malware is sufficient remediation (root cause must be identified and the attack vector closed)

name: Vulnerability Management
description: Vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, and patch management processes
key_facts: Vulnerability scans identify known weaknesses; penetration tests actively exploit them
CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) rates vulnerability severity from 0 to 10
Patch management includes testing patches before deployment and scheduling maintenance windows
False positives are reported vulnerabilities that do not actually exist; false negatives are missed real vulnerabilities
CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) provides standardized identifiers for known vulnerabilities
common_misconceptions: Vulnerability scanning and penetration testing are the same (scanning identifies issues; pentesting exploits them)
All critical patches should be deployed immediately (patches must be tested to avoid breaking systems)

name: Risk Management and Analysis
description: Risk assessment methodologies, risk treatment options, and business impact analysis
key_facts: Risk = Threat x Vulnerability x Impact
Risk treatment options: accept, avoid, transfer (insurance), mitigate (reduce likelihood or impact)
Quantitative risk: SLE = AV x EF; ALE = SLE x ARO (annualized loss expectancy)
Qualitative risk uses categories (high/medium/low) and risk matrices
BIA (Business Impact Analysis) identifies critical systems and their maximum tolerable downtime
common_misconceptions: All risks must be eliminated (some risks are accepted when mitigation cost exceeds potential loss)
Risk assessment is a one-time activity (it must be ongoing as threats and business needs change)

name: Compliance and Governance Frameworks
description: Regulatory compliance requirements, security frameworks, and security policies
key_facts: GDPR governs data privacy for EU citizens; requires consent and data breach notification within 72 hours
PCI DSS sets security standards for organizations handling credit card data
NIST Cybersecurity Framework: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
Security policies: AUP (acceptable use), data retention, incident response, change management
SOC 2 audits evaluate an organization's controls for security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy
common_misconceptions: Compliance equals security (compliance is a minimum baseline; organizations can be compliant and still insecure)
GDPR only applies to EU companies (it applies to any organization processing data of EU residents)
